themeBlurb: Word choice, collocations, idioms, connectors, register markers. Powers Paper 2 Booklet A (Vocab MCQ + Vocab Cloze) and lifts Paper 1 Composition register.

vocab.title: Vocabulary, Idioms & Collocations
vocab.blurb: Build precise word choice. 170+ collocations, 80+ idioms with frequency tags.
keyQuestions: Is this a verb-noun, adjective-noun, or verb-preposition collocation?
Is this idiom common, moderate, or rare? When is it the right choice?
Does this register match the genre (formal letter vs informal email)?
Does this connector match the function I need (adding, contrasting, causal, sequencing)?

glossary.definition: A pair of words that naturally go together. Saying 'heavy rain' (not 'strong rain') signals AL1 register.
glossary.template: We had _____ rain last night. (heavy / strong)

glossary.definition: A phrase whose meaning is not literal. AL1 writers use 1–2 idioms per Continuous Writing piece, used precisely.
glossary.template: He was _____ when he heard the news. (on cloud nine)

glossary.definition: A discourse marker that signals the relationship between ideas (adding, contrasting, causal, sequential).
glossary.template: It was raining; _____, the match continued. (however / therefore)

glossary.definition: Idioms are tagged common / moderate / rare. Drill the 20 highest-frequency first; reach for rare only when fit is perfect.
glossary.template: Common = 'on cloud nine'. Rare = 'between Scylla and Charybdis' — don't use.

glossary.definition: Formal vs informal vocabulary alternatives. The Cloze and Editing components also reward these.
glossary.template: get → obtain; ask → enquire; tell → inform; start → commence.

vocab.learnHTML:
Why vocabulary matters

Vocabulary is tested directly on Paper 2 Booklet A (5 Vocab MCQ + 5 Vocab Cloze = 10 marks) and indirectly through Composition register, SBC fluency, and Comprehension Cloze inference. 3–4 of the 15 Comprehension Cloze blanks each year are decided by collocation knowledge alone.

The four collocation categories (with the most-tested examples)

1. Verb + Noun:

MAKEDOCATCH / GET / RUN / HOLD
make a decisiondo homeworkcatch a cold / catch the bus
make a mistakedo researchget angry / get lost / get a job
make progressdo businessrun a business / run a risk
make an effortdo a favourhold a meeting / hold your breath
make a differencedo your bestkeep a promise / keep a secret

2. Adjective + Noun:

HEAVYSTRONGLIGHT / DEEP / HIGH
heavy rain / trafficstrong coffee / windlight rain / meal
heavy meal / smokerstrong opinion / evidencedeep sleep / regret / silence
heavy heart / workloadstrong personality / supporthigh standard / pressure / cost

3. Verb + Preposition: depend ON · rely ON · succeed IN · agree WITH · apologise FOR · insist ON · consist OF · suffer FROM · benefit FROM · participate IN.

4. Preposition + Noun (fixed phrases): in time / on time · at the moment · in the end · by mistake / on purpose · in danger · for ages · in advance · out of breath · at first / at last / in fact.

Fast / Quick / Rapid — the precision drill

These three words look similar but cannot be swapped freely. Knowing which goes with which noun is high-value AL1 vocabulary.

  • FAST: fast food, fast car, fast train, fast runner, fast asleep.
  • QUICK: quick glance, quick bite (meal), quick decision, quick shower, quick thinker.
  • RAPID: rapid growth, rapid change, rapid decline, rapid progress, rapid response.

Idiom usage map — when to use which

Tier 1 (drill first; safe to use): on cloud nine, burn the midnight oil, butterflies in stomach, break out in cold sweat, heart in mouth, with a heavy heart, nerves of steel, at a crossroads, blood sweat and tears, a blessing in disguise, in the nick of time, out of the blue, learn the hard way, practice makes perfect, a bolt from the blue, a heart of gold, go the extra mile, break the ice.

Tier 2 (versatile; use when fit is right): face the music, rise to the occasion, kill two birds with one stone, the last straw, read between the lines, hit the nail on the head.

Tier 3 (avoid in composition): raining cats and dogs (cliché — use "the rain poured down in sheets" instead), kick the bucket (flippant), piece of work (ambiguous), spill the beans (informal), over my dead body (hyperbolic).

Frequency rule: 1–2 idioms per Continuous Writing piece, not more. Each must serve the moment, not decorate.

Connectors by function (the 6 categories)

  • Adding: in addition, furthermore, moreover, also, besides, what is more.
  • Contrasting: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, although, despite, in contrast.
  • Causal: because, since, due to, owing to, thanks to, on account of.
  • Result: therefore, thus, consequently, as a result, hence, for this reason.
  • Sequential: first, next, then, meanwhile, eventually, suddenly, before long.
  • Concluding: in conclusion, to sum up, overall, ultimately, all in all.
Despite vs Although trap. Despite + noun phrase ("Despite the rain"). Although + clause ("Although it was raining"). Never mix.

Register swap pairs (formal upgrades)

In any formal letter, email, report, or article, replace these casual verbs with their formal alternatives. The Cloze and Editing components reward these too.

  • ask → enquire / request
  • help → assist / support
  • get → obtain / receive
  • tell → inform / notify
  • show → demonstrate / indicate
  • start → commence / initiate
  • end → conclude / terminate
  • try → attempt / endeavour
  • think → consider / believe
  • want → wish / require
  • need → require
  • give → provide / supply
  • find out → discover / ascertain
  • put off → postpone / defer
  • set up → establish / arrange
  • look into → investigate / examine
  • thanks → I appreciate / I am grateful
  • really → very / extremely
vocab.summaryHTML: Full banks live in Collocations_KB.md (170+ items), Idioms_Expanded_KB.md (80+ items frequency-tagged), Connectors_KB.md (60+ items by function), and Register_KB.md (genre-by-genre matrix).
pslePrompts: Common idioms first. Drill the 20 highest-frequency ones to automaticity before reaching for rare ones.
One idiom per Continuous Writing piece — when it fits the moment, not as decoration.
In a formal letter, replace 'get / ask / tell' with 'obtain / enquire / inform'.
Heavy rain, not strong rain. Strong coffee, not heavy coffee. Memorise the adjective-noun pairs.
Despite + noun phrase. Although + clause. Never mix.

commonTraps.wrong: "We had strong rain last night." / "She made a strong coffee."
commonTraps.fix: Heavy rain, strong coffee. Memorise the adjective-noun pairs as units.
commonTraps.why: Collocations are fixed by convention, not logic. Native English speakers will hear the mismatch instantly.

commonTraps.wrong: "He did a mistake." / "She made her homework."
commonTraps.fix: Make a mistake, do homework. The Make/Do split is the single most-tested collocation in PSLE Cloze.
commonTraps.why: Make = create / produce / cause (decision, progress, effort). Do = perform / complete / occupy (homework, research, business).

commonTraps.wrong: "Although it rained, but the match continued."
commonTraps.fix: Use ONE connector, not both: "Although it rained, the match continued" OR "It rained, but the match continued".
commonTraps.why: Double-up connectors are a common Editing trap. Pick one — the second is redundant.

commonTraps.wrong: "Despite of the rain, the match continued." / "Despite it was raining, the match continued."
commonTraps.fix: Despite + noun phrase (no 'of'): "Despite the rain, the match continued."
commonTraps.why: Despite never takes 'of', and never takes a clause. The structure is fixed.

commonTraps.wrong: Stuffing every paragraph with idioms ("He was on cloud nine, with butterflies in his stomach, walking on air…").
commonTraps.fix: 1–2 idioms per piece, each landing a specific moment.
commonTraps.why: Idiom overuse signals memorised templates and lacks discernment. AL1 picks idioms surgically.

commonTraps.wrong: Singapore-English slips in formal writing: "He revert by tomorrow" / "Open the aircon".
commonTraps.fix: "He will reply by tomorrow" / "Turn on the air-conditioner". The Cloze rewards standard English over Singlish.
commonTraps.why: Singapore-specific verbs (revert = reply; chop = stamp; open the light = turn on) cost marks in formal PSLE writing.

masteryCheckpoints: You know 15+ Verb+Noun collocations by heart and can spot 'make a mistake' vs 'do a mistake' instantly.
You can explain the Heavy / Strong / Fast / Quick distinction with at least one example each.
You can use 1–2 idioms per composition piece naturally, not as decoration.
You know the swap pairs (ask→enquire, get→obtain, tell→inform) and apply them in formal writing.
You can name the 6 connector functions (Adding, Contrasting, Causal, Result, Sequential, Concluding) and give 2 connectors for each.
You never mix Despite (noun phrase) with Although (clause).
You vary sentence openers and avoid "Then… Then… Then…" chains.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the natural collocation: "We had _____ rain last night."
drillItems.explanation: 'Heavy rain' is the fixed adjective-noun collocation.

drillItems.prompt: Choose: "She drank _____ coffee to stay awake."
drillItems.explanation: 'Strong coffee', not 'heavy coffee'. Heavy/Strong split is the most-tested adjective collocation.

drillItems.prompt: Make or Do? "She _____ a difficult decision."
drillItems.explanation: Make a decision. Make = create / produce (decision, mistake, effort).

drillItems.prompt: Make or Do? "He needs to _____ his homework before dinner."
drillItems.explanation: Do homework. Do = perform / complete (homework, research, business).

drillItems.prompt: Choose the natural verb: "When the news broke, she _____ into tears."
drillItems.explanation: 'Burst into tears' is the fixed collocation. Also: burst into laughter / burst into flames.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the idiom for terrified at a sudden moment:
drillItems.explanation: 'Heart in my mouth' = terrified at a sudden moment. Tier-1 high-frequency PSLE idiom.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the connector for contrast: "It was raining. _____, the match continued."
drillItems.explanation: 'However' marks contrast. Therefore = result; Because = cause; Finally = sequence.

drillItems.prompt: Despite or Although? "_____ the heavy rain, the football match continued."
drillItems.explanation: Despite + noun phrase (Despite the rain). Although + clause (Although it was raining). Never 'despite of'.
Get → obtain (or receive). Formal letter / report / article register.
Ask → enquire (or request). Used in formal letters and reports.

drillItems.prompt: Fast / Quick / Rapid? "She made a _____ glance at her watch."
drillItems.explanation: 'Quick glance' is the natural pair. Fast pairs with food/cars; rapid pairs with growth/change.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the AL2 → AL1 upgrade for 'a lot of money':
drillItems.explanation: AL1 register: 'a great deal of money' or 'a considerable sum'. 'A lot of' is conversational.

questions.question: We had _____ rain last night.
questions.explanation: 'Heavy rain' is the natural collocation in English. 'Strong rain' is grammatically possible but unnatural — examiners reward the standard pair.

questions.question: After hours of practice, she finally _____ a breakthrough.
questions.explanation: 'Make a breakthrough' is the collocation. Other 'make' pairings to learn: make a decision, make progress, make an effort, make a difference.

questions.question: When the principal called his name, his _____.
questions.explanation: The idiom 'heart in (one's) mouth' means terrified at a sudden moment. It is one of the 20 highest-frequency idioms at PSLE level and fits this context exactly.

questions.question: Which sentence uses 'Despite' correctly?
options: Despite of the rain, the match continued.
Despite it was raining, the match continued.
Despite the heavy rain, the match continued.
Despite although it was raining, the match continued.
questions.explanation: Despite is always followed by a NOUN PHRASE — never a clause and never 'of'. 'Despite the heavy rain' is correct; the alternatives mis-structure the connector.

questions.question: Which is a formal vocabulary upgrade ('swap pair')?
questions.explanation: Swap pairs upgrade casual verbs to formal alternatives. ask→enquire/request, get→obtain, tell→inform, start→commence are the high-frequency upgrades used in formal letters, reports, and articles.
